blocks.content: I found it on the first night.
In the front pocket of his bag, folded in quarters. My handwriting. Eight years old and still there.
I had chosen every word carefully. I had written it and rewritten it and folded it and given it to someone I trusted completely. And that person kept it.

Eight years. I sat across tables from him. I flew to his wedding. I watched everything I had asked for in that letter become someone else's life — and I thought he had said no. I thought he had decided. I thought he knew and chose not to answer.
He never saw it because it was here. In a front pocket, folded in quarters, carried for eight years by the one person who should have handed it over.
I held it for a long time. The paper had gone soft at the folds. Eight years in a pocket will do that to anything.
I don't know why. I may never know why. What I know is that he kept it, and eight years passed, and the person I wrote it for never received a single word.

I have been patient for eight years. I have been careful. I have been the most controlled person in every room I've walked into since the day I stopped waiting for a response that was never going to come.
And now I am standing in the dark at the top of the stairs, and the house is quiet, and I have made a decision.

blocks.prompt: Something about these words stays with you. What do you hold onto?
